title: Pituitary Apoplexy
oneLiner: Haemorrhage or infarction of a (usually undiagnosed) pituitary adenoma causing sudden headache, visual loss, and life-threatening acute hypopituitarism.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Adrenal crisis from ACTH deficiency** → refractory hypotension, hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia. *Key finding: shock that does not respond to fluids alone + low random cortisol (<100 nmol/L)*. **This kills before imaging.**
- **Cavernous sinus thrombosis / carotid occlusion** → proptosis, chemosis, cranial nerve palsies (III, IV, VI, V1, V2). *Key finding: fever + chemosis + CN signs — not typical of simple apoplexy, but mimics septic cavernous sinus lesion and can cause stroke.*
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - Sudden severe (“thunderclap”) headache – retro-orbital or frontal
  - Nausea / vomiting (meningism may be present)
  - Visual loss: acute or subacute, often bitemporal hemianopia
  - Diplopia (CN III most common) – look for ptosis, mydriasis, “down-and-out” eye
  - Signs of hypopituitarism: hypotension, pallor, loss of axillary/pubic hair (chronic)
- Key exam findings:
  - Assess visual acuity, colour vision, fields (bedside confrontation – bitemporal defect is classic)
  - Pupils: relative afferent defect if optic nerve is compressed
  - Extraocular movements: CN III, IV, VI palsy
  - Neuro: GCS, meningism (if blood in subarachnoid space)
  - General: lying/standing BP, capillary glucose
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH)** → CT head (blood), LP (xanthochromia); no CN palsies unless posterior communicating aneurysm
- **Migraine** → gradual onset, no focal neurology, no meningism
- **Meningitis / encephalitis** → fever, neck stiffness, CSF pleocytosis; CN palsies less common and usually later
- **Cavernous sinus thrombosis** → fever, periorbital oedema, chemosis, unilateral CN III/IV/VI; CT venography shows filling defect
- **Brainstem stroke** → sudden diplopia but also long tract signs (contralateral weakness, ataxia); vertigo common
- **Splitter:** “Sudden headache + bilateral visual loss (bitemporal) + CN III palsy” → **pituitary apoplexy until proven otherwise** – SAH does not cause bilateral visual loss with diplopia.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Capillary glucose (hypoglycaemia = crisis)
  - Lying & standing BP (postural drop = adrenal insufficiency)
  - Formal visual fields by confrontation
- **Bloods (urgent panels):**
  - U&E (hyponatraemia common), glucose (low), cortisol (random – if <100 nmol/L suggests crisis – **do not wait for result to give steroids**)
  - ACTH, TFT, prolactin, LH, FSH, IGF-1 (send but do not delay management)
  - FBC, coagulation, group & save (if surgical)
  - Blood cultures if fever (rule out meningitis)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **non-contrast CT head** (may show hyperdense sellar mass or haemorrhage; normal CT does NOT exclude apoplexy)
  - Second-line: **MRI pituitary with contrast** (gold standard – reveals haemorrhage/infarction within adenoma)
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Random serum cortisol** (urgent) + **MRI pituitary** (if CT normal and clinical suspicion high)
  - **Pregnancy test** in any woman of reproductive age (pituitary apoplexy can be precipitated by pregnancy – Sheehan’s syndrome variant)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABC** – airway may be threatened if GCS <9; O2 to sat >94%
  - IV access × 2; send bloods before steroids
  - **Hydrocortisone 100mg IV stat** (see Doses) – do NOT wait for cortisol result
  - **Fluid resuscitation:** 0.9% saline IV bolus (500mL → reassess; repeat to target MAP >65 mmHg)
  - **Treat hypoglycaemia:** 50mL IV 50% glucose if needed
  - **Consult neurosurgery & endocrinology** immediately
  - **Urgent MRI pituitary** (if CT negative but clinical suspicion high)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue hydrocortisone 50mg IV q6h (or per local protocol) for 24-48h then switch to oral
  - Monitor hourly: GCS, pupils, visual fields, BP, urine output, Na⁺
  - Assess for diabetes insipidus (polyuria, hypernatraemia) – check fluid balance, urine osmolality
  - Replace thyroxine **only after** adrenal coverage (to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis)
  - If visual deterioration or neurological decline → urgent trans-sphenoidal decompression
  - **SIADH** may develop (hyponatraemia) – avoid hypotonic fluids; treat with fluid restriction if severe
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifelong endocrine follow-up: reassess anterior pituitary function at 6 weeks and 3 months
  - MRI every 6-12 months for residual tumour surveillance
  - Consider radiotherapy if residual tumour grows or aggressive features
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline at 125 mL/hr initially, adjust for Na⁺
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin [check local policy] (controversial if haemorrhagic; defer to neurosurgery)
  - Diet/NBM: NBM initially (surgical candidate); then normal if no intervention
  - Observations: q1h GCS, pupils, BP, HR; q4h Na⁺, glucose, urine output
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (adrenal crisis): Hydrocortisone 100mg IV stat, then 50mg IV q6h** (or 100mg IV then 200mg/24h continuous infusion) – for 24-48h, then taper to oral (e.g., 20mg AM, 10mg PM)
- **Common alternative:** Dexamethasone 8-16mg IV stat (if cerebral oedema concern; less mineralocorticoid effect)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Do NOT give thyroxine before hydrocortisone – precipitates adrenal crisis
  - NSAIDs increase GI bleed risk if haemorrhagic component
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for hydrocortisone – no dose change
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Omitting the first dose of hydrocortisone** – patient will die from adrenal crisis. Second: giving thyroxine before adrenal coverage.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Adrenal crisis (hypotension, hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia)
  - Permanent visual loss (if decompression delayed >48h)
  - Cranial nerve palsy (usually recovers)
  - CSF leak after surgery
  - Diabetes insipidus (transient or permanent)
  - SIADH (hyponatraemia)
- **Chronic:**
  - Panhypopituitarism (most patients require lifelong replacement)
  - Tumour recurrence (frequency varies by histology)
  - Empty sella syndrome
sections.Prognosis:
- Mortality <5% if diagnosed and treated promptly; unrecognised adrenal crisis is fatal.
- Most patients regain full visual function if surgery within 48h but permanent hormone deficiencies are common (70-80% require multiple replacements).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Give hydrocortisone IMMEDIATELY** – before scanning, before neurosurgery review – even before cortisol is back.
- **For any thunderclap headache + visual loss,** check visual fields at the bedside – bitemporal loss is pathognomonic for sellar pathology.
- **Screen for hypopituitarism in all unexplained hypotension** – ask about prior headache/visual episodes and check random cortisol.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not delay steroids because you suspect SAH** – steroids are not harmful in SAH (actually used in some protocols) and delaying is fatal in apoplexy.
- **Do not treat isolated hyponatraemia with hypertonic saline without checking cortisol first** – in adrenal crisis, steroids + fluids correct the Na⁺.
- **Do not discharge a patient with “migraine” and mild diplopia** – apoplexy can present with gradual onset; always check visual fields and cortisol.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** worsening headache, any new visual change (blurring, field loss), seizure, confusion, or symptoms of adrenal insufficiency (dizziness, fatigue, vomiting, profound weakness)
- **Follow-up:** urgent endocrine clinic within 2-4 weeks; neurosurgery if residual tumour; repeat MRI at 3 months
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior (med reg / endocrinologist):** any suspicion of pituitary apoplexy – call immediately (do not wait for imaging)
- **When to call ICU / neurosurgery:**
  - GCS drop by ≥2 points or <9
  - Visual deterioration (acuity or field)
  - Haemodynamic instability despite fluids and steroids
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L
- **Specific thresholds:** GCS ≤13, BP <90/60 despite 2L crystalloid + steroids, Na⁺ <125 mmol/L or >155 mmol/L

rawMarkdown:
## Pituitary Apoplexy
**One-liner:** Haemorrhage or infarction of a (usually undiagnosed) pituitary adenoma causing sudden headache, visual loss, and life-threatening acute hypopituitarism.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Adrenal crisis from ACTH deficiency** → refractory hypotension, hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia. *Key finding: shock that does not respond to fluids alone + low random cortisol (<100 nmol/L)*. **This kills before imaging.**
- **Cavernous sinus thrombosis / carotid occlusion** → proptosis, chemosis, cranial nerve palsies (III, IV, VI, V1, V2). *Key finding: fever + chemosis + CN signs — not typical of simple apoplexy, but mimics septic cavernous sinus lesion and can cause stroke.*

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - Sudden severe (“thunderclap”) headache – retro-orbital or frontal
  - Nausea / vomiting (meningism may be present)
  - Visual loss: acute or subacute, often bitemporal hemianopia
  - Diplopia (CN III most common) – look for ptosis, mydriasis, “down-and-out” eye
  - Signs of hypopituitarism: hypotension, pallor, loss of axillary/pubic hair (chronic)
- Key exam findings:
  - Assess visual acuity, colour vision, fields (bedside confrontation – bitemporal defect is classic)
  - Pupils: relative afferent defect if optic nerve is compressed
  - Extraocular movements: CN III, IV, VI palsy
  - Neuro: GCS, meningism (if blood in subarachnoid space)
  - General: lying/standing BP, capillary glucose

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH)** → CT head (blood), LP (xanthochromia); no CN palsies unless posterior communicating aneurysm
- **Migraine** → gradual onset, no focal neurology, no meningism
- **Meningitis / encephalitis** → fever, neck stiffness, CSF pleocytosis; CN palsies less common and usually later
- **Cavernous sinus thrombosis** → fever, periorbital oedema, chemosis, unilateral CN III/IV/VI; CT venography shows filling defect
- **Brainstem stroke** → sudden diplopia but also long tract signs (contralateral weakness, ataxia); vertigo common
- **Splitter:** “Sudden headache + bilateral visual loss (bitemporal) + CN III palsy” → **pituitary apoplexy until proven otherwise** – SAH does not cause bilateral visual loss with diplopia.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Capillary glucose (hypoglycaemia = crisis)
  - Lying & standing BP (postural drop = adrenal insufficiency)
  - Formal visual fields by confrontation
- **Bloods (urgent panels):**
  - U&E (hyponatraemia common), glucose (low), cortisol (random – if <100 nmol/L suggests crisis – **do not wait for result to give steroids**)
  - ACTH, TFT, prolactin, LH, FSH, IGF-1 (send but do not delay management)
  - FBC, coagulation, group & save (if surgical)
  - Blood cultures if fever (rule out meningitis)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **non-contrast CT head** (may show hyperdense sellar mass or haemorrhage; normal CT does NOT exclude apoplexy)
  - Second-line: **MRI pituitary with contrast** (gold standard – reveals haemorrhage/infarction within adenoma)
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Random serum cortisol** (urgent) + **MRI pituitary** (if CT normal and clinical suspicion high)
  - **Pregnancy test** in any woman of reproductive age (pituitary apoplexy can be precipitated by pregnancy – Sheehan’s syndrome variant)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABC** – airway may be threatened if GCS <9; O2 to sat >94%
  - IV access × 2; send bloods before steroids
  - **Hydrocortisone 100mg IV stat** (see Doses) – do NOT wait for cortisol result
  - **Fluid resuscitation:** 0.9% saline IV bolus (500mL → reassess; repeat to target MAP >65 mmHg)
  - **Treat hypoglycaemia:** 50mL IV 50% glucose if needed
  - **Consult neurosurgery & endocrinology** immediately
  - **Urgent MRI pituitary** (if CT negative but clinical suspicion high)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue hydrocortisone 50mg IV q6h (or per local protocol) for 24-48h then switch to oral
  - Monitor hourly: GCS, pupils, visual fields, BP, urine output, Na⁺
  - Assess for diabetes insipidus (polyuria, hypernatraemia) – check fluid balance, urine osmolality
  - Replace thyroxine **only after** adrenal coverage (to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis)
  - If visual deterioration or neurological decline → urgent trans-sphenoidal decompression
  - **SIADH** may develop (hyponatraemia) – avoid hypotonic fluids; treat with fluid restriction if severe
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifelong endocrine follow-up: reassess anterior pituitary function at 6 weeks and 3 months
  - MRI every 6-12 months for residual tumour surveillance
  - Consider radiotherapy if residual tumour grows or aggressive features
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline at 125 mL/hr initially, adjust for Na⁺
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin [check local policy] (controversial if haemorrhagic; defer to neurosurgery)
  - Diet/NBM: NBM initially (surgical candidate); then normal if no intervention
  - Observations: q1h GCS, pupils, BP, HR; q4h Na⁺, glucose, urine output

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (adrenal crisis): Hydrocortisone 100mg IV stat, then 50mg IV q6h** (or 100mg IV then 200mg/24h continuous infusion) – for 24-48h, then taper to oral (e.g., 20mg AM, 10mg PM)
- **Common alternative:** Dexamethasone 8-16mg IV stat (if cerebral oedema concern; less mineralocorticoid effect)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Do NOT give thyroxine before hydrocortisone – precipitates adrenal crisis
  - NSAIDs increase GI bleed risk if haemorrhagic component
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None for hydrocortisone – no dose change
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Omitting the first dose of hydrocortisone** – patient will die from adrenal crisis. Second: giving thyroxine before adrenal coverage.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Adrenal crisis (hypotension, hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia)
  - Permanent visual loss (if decompression delayed >48h)
  - Cranial nerve palsy (usually recovers)
  - CSF leak after surgery
  - Diabetes insipidus (transient or permanent)
  - SIADH (hyponatraemia)
- **Chronic:**
  - Panhypopituitarism (most patients require lifelong replacement)
  - Tumour recurrence (frequency varies by histology)
  - Empty sella syndrome

### Prognosis
- Mortality <5% if diagnosed and treated promptly; unrecognised adrenal crisis is fatal.
- Most patients regain full visual function if surgery within 48h but permanent hormone deficiencies are common (70-80% require multiple replacements).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Give hydrocortisone IMMEDIATELY** – before scanning, before neurosurgery review – even before cortisol is back.
- **For any thunderclap headache + visual loss,** check visual fields at the bedside – bitemporal loss is pathognomonic for sellar pathology.
- **Screen for hypopituitarism in all unexplained hypotension** – ask about prior headache/visual episodes and check random cortisol.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not delay steroids because you suspect SAH** – steroids are not harmful in SAH (actually used in some protocols) and delaying is fatal in apoplexy.
- **Do not treat isolated hyponatraemia with hypertonic saline without checking cortisol first** – in adrenal crisis, steroids + fluids correct the Na⁺.
- **Do not discharge a patient with “migraine” and mild diplopia** – apoplexy can present with gradual onset; always check visual fields and cortisol.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** worsening headache, any new visual change (blurring, field loss), seizure, confusion, or symptoms of adrenal insufficiency (dizziness, fatigue, vomiting, profound weakness)
- **Follow-up:** urgent endocrine clinic within 2-4 weeks; neurosurgery if residual tumour; repeat MRI at 3 months

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior (med reg / endocrinologist):** any suspicion of pituitary apoplexy – call immediately (do not wait for imaging)
- **When to call ICU / neurosurgery:**
  - GCS drop by ≥2 points or <9
  - Visual deterioration (acuity or field)
  - Haemodynamic instability despite fluids and steroids
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L
- **Specific thresholds:** GCS ≤13, BP <90/60 despite 2L crystalloid + steroids, Na⁺ <125 mmol/L or >155 mmol/L
